w:r.w:t: Stack: Go backend, React frontend, PostgreSQL database, Docker Compose runtime
Testing date: TODO: fill in final execution date/time if required
Test environment: TODO: fill in machine CPU/RAM/OS details if required

The application performance was analysed using Go profiling and k6 load testing. The Go backend was configured to write file-based pprof CPU and heap profiles. k6 was used to simulate concurrent users against the REST service.

Profiler used: Go runtime/pprof. Profiling was implemented without exposing debug HTTP endpoints. The backend writes profile files during a normal Docker Compose run and flushes them on graceful shutdown.
Profile files used in this report:
Note: profiles/manual-cpu.pprof was empty in the latest folder listing, so the non-empty CPU profile manual-cpu01.pprof was used for the report.
a. Manually Execute Main Test Cases From Test Plan
Manual scenarios were defined in docs/performance-test-plan.md and executed while the profiler was active. The important tested flows were:
- Play coinflip, dice, roulette, slots, blackjack, crash, mines, and plinko
- Open profile, settings, missions, achievements, and notifications
TODO: if required by the teacher, add exact manual scenario execution order, screenshots, or timestamps.
CPU usage was measured with Go pprof. The CPU profile was analysed with: go tool pprof -top profiles/manual-cpu01.pprof

- Top flat CPU entries: runtime.futex 160 ms (43.24%), internal/runtime/syscall/linux.Syscall6 120 ms (32.43%), runtime.write1 20 ms (5.41%), reflect.Value.IsNil 10 ms (2.70%), runtime.mallocgc 10 ms flat / 30 ms cumulative (8.11% cumulative).

Interpretation: the sampled CPU time was dominated by Go runtime and syscall/waiting behavior, not by one application game method. This suggests that during the profiled run the backend spent much of the sampled time waiting on OS/runtime synchronization or I/O rather than burning CPU in custom business logic.
TODO: insert CPU pprof flame graph screenshot if the report requires visual evidence.
i. Discover Methods That Consume Most CPU Time

The top CPU-consuming sampled functions were runtime.futex, internal/runtime/syscall/linux.Syscall6, runtime.write1, reflect.Value.IsNil, and runtime.mallocgc. No custom application handler dominated CPU samples in the collected profile.

Conclusion: based on this CPU profile, the main performance risk was not a single CPU-heavy game calculation method. The more relevant bottlenecks appear to be request volume, database access, JSON serialization, and allocation pressure, which are more visible in memory/allocation profiling and load-test response times.
Memory usage was measured with Go heap profiling. A heap snapshot was written on backend shutdown and analysed with: go tool pprof -top -inuse_space profiles/manual-mem02.pprof

Interpretation: the memory retained at the snapshot moment was mostly related to buffered HTTP connection readers/writers and runtime allocations. This matches an HTTP backend under concurrent request load.
i. Take Memory Snapshot After/During Execution Or Test Cases
A memory snapshot was taken after executing test cases by writing a Go heap profile during graceful backend shutdown. This snapshot is represented by profiles/manual-mem02.pprof.
TODO: insert memory pprof graph/flame graph screenshot if visual evidence is required.
ii. Discover Most Allocated Object And Memory Consumed

Go pprof reports allocations mainly by allocating function/call path rather than Java-style object class names. Allocation volume was analysed with: go tool pprof -top -alloc_space profiles/manual-mem02.pprof

Interpretation: the highest allocation pressure came from achievements, missions, notifications, and pgx database row scanning. These are not necessarily memory leaks; alloc_space measures total allocation churn over time, including memory later freed by the garbage collector.
Most allocated application-level paths: achievement scanning/loading, mission scanning/loading, notification loading, and database scan operations.

Load/stress testing was performed with k6 using the mixed scenario in loadtest/mixed.js. The scenario uses the REST API and simulates users loading state, topping up, opening profile, and playing games such as coinflip, dice, roulette, slots, and plinko.
a. Simulate 1000 Simultaneous Users For 5 Minutes
The k6 test was executed with 1000 virtual users for 5 minutes. The k6 output reported 1000 max VUs and a 5m0s scenario duration.

To avoid local Docker/Windows TCP connection-refused errors from an instant startup burst, the script uses realistic user pacing: each virtual user has a think time, and the first request is jittered. This still simulates 1000 simultaneous users because 1000 VUs are active during the run.
b. Measure Response Times And Throughput
k6 measured response times and throughput. Results from profiles/loadtest-results.json and the k6 console output:

Interpretation: the backend remained available during the 1000-user test with 0 failed HTTP requests. Throughput was about 256 requests per second. Median response time was around 1 second, but tail latency was higher, with p95 at about 3.06 seconds and the slowest request at 18.74 seconds.
- CPU profile did not show a single custom application method dominating CPU time.
- Memory allocation pressure was concentrated in achievement scanning/loading, mission scanning/loading, notification loading, and pgx database scanning.
- Load testing showed successful availability under 1000 users, but response times had noticeable tail latency.
- The application may benefit from reducing repeated mission/achievement/notification loading, optimizing database query patterns, and avoiding unnecessary allocations during request handling.

The lab goals were completed for the Go application. The backend was profiled for CPU and memory usage, a heap memory snapshot was collected, allocation-heavy functions were identified, and a 1000-user REST load test was executed for 5 minutes. The application handled the load test without HTTP request failures, reaching about 256 requests per second. The main improvement area is allocation pressure in repeated state/progress loading paths and long-tail response latency under concurrent load.
